sourceRef.citation: Kourovka Notebook, Problem 11.128 (Khukhro & Mazurov eds., ed.11)

statement.latex: A group$G$is said to be a$K$-group\/ if for every subgroup$A G$there exists a subgroup$B G$such that$A B=1$and$< A,B > =G$. Is it true that normal sub\-groups of$K$-groups are also$K$-groups? { M.\,Emaldi} No, it is not (V.\,N.\,Obraztsov, J.~Austral. Math. Soc. (Ser.~A), { 61}, no.\,2 (1996), 267--288).

reiAssessment.solveRationale: Group theory I_INFINITE_SEARCH; Rei not specialized in group theory (yet)
reiAssessment.formalizationNotes: Kourovka problems are typically research-level group theory
